1: wayyitkazzēq šᵉlōmōh — ‘and Solomon strengthened himself’ or ‘established himself firmly’; Hithpael of ḥāzaq (to be strong, to hold fast), the reflexive of the standard strength-verb. The Hithpael form makes Solomon the agent of his own strengthening — not merely passive recipient of stability but active self-consolidation. The Chronicler uses this Hithpael form at key transition moments: 1 Chr 11:10 (David’s mighty men who ‘held strongly with him’); 2 Chr 12:13 (Rehoboam ‘strengthened himself’ in Jerusalem); 2 Chr 32:5 (Hezekiah ‘took courage’ and rebuilt the wall). Each instance marks a king’s intentional consolidation of covenant rule. The MKT renders ‘established himself firmly,’ capturing the reflexive will-to-stability implied by the Hithpael. The clause immediately qualifies: ‘waYHWH ʾelōhāyw ʿimmô’ — ‘and YHWH his God was with him.’ The divine-presence clause follows and grounds the human self-consolidation: Solomon strengthens himself because YHWH is with him, not apart from that presence.
7: šᵉʾal lāk — ‘Ask for yourself’; the divine invitation to make a request. The verb šāʾal (to ask, request, inquire) is the lexical root of the name šāʾûl (Saul), meaning ‘asked/requested [of God].’ The irony is structurally embedded: the king named ‘Asked-of-God’ failed to maintain the covenant; now God invites the new king with the same verb — ‘Ask [šᵉʾal] for yourself, what I shall give you.’ Solomon asks for ḥokmāh (wisdom) and maddāʿ (knowledge/discernment) — the same pairing appears in Dan 1:17 for Daniel and his companions (‘God gave them knowledge and skill in all literature and wisdom,’ yādaʿ and ḥokmāh). The MKT renders ‘wisdom and knowledge,’ maintaining the two-term structure. The Chronicler adds maddāʿ to the 1 Kgs 3:9 account (‘hearing heart’), shifting from the relational-wisdom (‘heart’) emphasis to the cognitive-practical (‘knowledge’) emphasis suitable for the administrative-organizational king the Chronicler portrays Solomon to be.
12: wᵉhahôn wᵉhāʿōšer wᵉhakkāḇôḏ — ‘and wealth and riches and honor’; three nouns added by the Chronicler beyond the parallel in 1 Kgs 3:13 (‘riches and honor’). The Chronicler’s expansion makes explicit that secondary blessings are the consequence of wisdom-first priority — a pedagogical addition consistent with the Chronicler’s consistent prosperity-through-covenant-faithfulness theology. hôn (wealth, substance) appears frequently in Proverbs (1:13; 3:9; 10:15) as the wealth that comes through wise living. ʿōšer (riches, accumulated wealth) is the broader material term. kāḇôḏ (honor, glory, weightiness) is the social-standing dimension. The three together constitute the comprehensive Solomonic blessing: material, financial, and social.
2: wayyō'mer šᵉlōmōh lᵉkol-yiśrāʾēl — ‘Solomon addressed all Israel.’ The Chronicler’s signature concern is pan-Israel: the addressees are not merely Judah or the court but ‘all Israel’ — commanders, judges, and every leader throughout all Israel. The Chronicler uses kol-yiśrāʾēl (all Israel) as a formulaic emphasis that frames the ideal monarchy as encompassing the full covenant people. This contrasts with the divided-kingdom reality the Chronicler’s audience knew — the ‘all Israel’ ideal is a theological claim about the unity of the covenant community under a faithful Davidic king, projecting both backward to the historical ideal and forward to the hoped-for restoration.
3: bāmāh ʾăšer bᵉgiḇʿôn — ‘the high place at Gibeon.’ The Gibeon high place is legitimized by the presence of the miškan (tabernacle): the Mosaic tent of meeting was stationed there (1 Chr 16:39; 21:29). The Chronicler carefully explains why Solomon worships at a bāmāh (high place) — normally a suspect site in the Deuteronomistic evaluation scheme — without theological compromise: Gibeon held the authentic Mosaic sanctuary. The ark, however, was separately housed in David’s tent in Jerusalem (v. 4). The anomaly of two sanctuary sites (Gibeon and Jerusalem) is resolved by Solomon’s temple-building: the two loci are unified in the single permanent sanctuary.
4: wᵉʾarôn hāʾelōhîm heʿĕlāh dāwîḏ — ‘The ark of God, however, David had brought up.’ The adversative waw (and/but) marks the contrast: the tabernacle was at Gibeon (v. 3), but the ark was in Jerusalem. This separation — ark in Jerusalem, tabernacle in Gibeon — is the Chronicler’s explanation for why Solomon worships at Gibeon before the temple is built: the official Mosaic cultus was there, not at the politically prominent Jerusalem ark-tent. The ark’s presence in Jerusalem (brought by David, 1 Chr 15-16) anticipates the temple’s location: the ark drives the temple site rather than the tabernacle.
5: mizbah haneḥōšet ʾăšer ʿāśāh bᵉṣalʾēl ben-ûrî ben-ḥûr — ‘The bronze altar that Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, had made.’ The altar’s genealogy — traced to Bezalel, the Spirit-filled craftsman of the tabernacle (Exod 31:2; 35:30; 36:1) — authenticates its Mosaic legitimacy. The Chronicler’s specific mention of Bezalel connects Solomon’s pre-temple worship to the original Sinaitic appointment: the same altar, made by the Spirit-filled craftsman at Sinai, is still functioning at Gibeon generations later. The MKT retains the full genealogy (son of Uri, son of Hur) to preserve this line of authorization.
6: wayʿal šᵉlōmōh šāmmāh ʿal mizbah haneḥōšet... wayʿal ʿālāyw ʾelep ʿōlôt — ‘Solomon went up to the bronze altar... and offered a thousand burnt offerings on it.’ A thousand (ʾelep) burnt offerings is a round-number expression for extravagant worship — the superlative of devotion offered at the authorized Mosaic altar before the divine appearance. The ʿōlāh (burnt offering) is entirely consumed by fire; unlike the šᵉlāmîm (peace offering) where portions are shared, the ʿōlāh gives everything to YHWH. Solomon’s thousand offerings establish his devotion as total-gift worship, appropriate preparation for the divine encounter that follows that night.
8: ʾattāh ʿāśîtā ʿim-dāwîḏ ʾābî ḥesed gāḏôl — ‘You have shown great steadfast love to my father David.’ Solomon’s prayer begins with ḥesed acknowledgment before making any request — the covenantal posture of thanksgiving grounding petition. ḥesed gāḏôl (great steadfast love) is the superlative of covenantal faithfulness: not merely reliable but extravagantly loyal. The shift from David’s covenant-relationship to Solomon’s kingship is described as YHWH’s ḥesed continuing across generations — the Davidic covenant is a ḥesed-covenant, its continuity grounded in YHWH’s own character rather than human merit.
9: yeʾāmen dᵉḇar-kā ʿim-dāwîḏ ʾābî — ‘Let your promise to my father David be confirmed (yeʾāmen).’ The root is ʾāman — the same root as ʾāmēn (so be it) and ʾemûnāh (faithfulness). Solomon asks that YHWH’s word be made firm, established, reliable — the Niphal of ʾāman meaning to be confirmed, proven trustworthy. The Niphal form reverses the more common Hiphil (to believe/trust): Solomon isn’t asking whether he should believe YHWH but asking YHWH to bring his own word to completion. This is covenant-confidence prayer: not doubting the promise but asking for its actualization. kî attāh himlaktanî ʿal-ʿam rāb kaʿăpar hāʾāreṣ (for you have made me king over a people as numerous as the dust of the earth) — the dust-multiplication hyperbole echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 13:16; 28:14), framing Solomon’s kingship as the covenant’s fulfillment.
10: ḥokmāh ûmaddāʿ tēn-lî — ‘Give me wisdom and knowledge.’ The Chronicler’s version adds maddāʿ (knowledge, discernment) to the ḥokmāh (wisdom) of the 1 Kgs 3:9 parallel (‘hearing heart’). maddāʿ is the noun form of yāḏaʿ (to know) — specifically cognitive knowledge, the capacity to perceive and discern. The pairing of ḥokmāh (practical wisdom, skill in living) + maddāʿ (intellectual knowledge) appears in Dan 1:17 for Daniel and his companions, establishing a Second-Temple pattern for the ideal leader. wᵉʾēṣeʾāh lipnê hāʿam hazzeh wᵉʾābôʾāh (that I may lead this people) — the going-out and coming-in formula for military-leadership competence (Deut 31:2; Num 27:17), now applied to civic governance.
11: yaʿan ʾăšer hāyᵉtāh zōʾt bᵉlibbᵉkā — ‘Because this was in your heart.’ God’s response evaluates the inner motivation, not merely the content of the request. yaʿan ʾăšer (because, for the reason that) — a causal conjunction introducing YHWH’s explanation of why he grants the request. The heart-motivation is decisive: Solomon did not ask for riches, honor, or the death of enemies — negative categories that reveal what lesser leaders would have prioritized. The list — ʿōšer nᵉkāsîm wᵉkāḇôḏ wᵉnepheš śōnᵉʾekā (riches, possessions, honor, the life of your enemies) — is the Chronicler’s expansion beyond 1 Kgs 3:11 (which omits ‘possessions’). Each element is a self-serving alternative that Solomon rejected; his choice of governance-wisdom over personal advancement reveals his character.
13: wayyābōʾ šᵉlōmōh lāmāqôm habbāmāh ʾăšer bᵉgiḇʿôn yᵉrûšālaim — ‘Solomon returned from the high place at Gibeon — from before the tent of meeting — to Jerusalem.’ The phrase ‘from before the tent of meeting’ (millipnê ʾohel môʿēḏ) confirms that the Gibeon worship was before the official Mosaic sanctuary. The return to Jerusalem marks the end of the Gibeon-era worship: after this, all the building energy is directed toward the permanent temple that will make both Gibeon and David’s ark-tent obsolete. The narrative pivot — divine encounter at Gibeon followed by immediate return to Jerusalem — frames Solomon’s reign as moving from the provisional Mosaic arrangement to the permanent Davidic-Solomonic one.
14: wayyāʾsop šᵉlōmōh rekeb ûpārāšîm — ‘Solomon built up a force of chariots and cavalry.’ The military accumulation — fourteen hundred chariots, twelve thousand horsemen — is reported by the Chronicler without explicit evaluation, but the Deuteronomic law of the king (Deut 17:16) warned: ‘he must not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire many horses.’ Solomon’s horses were imported from Egypt (v. 16), precisely the scenario Deuteronomy prohibits. The Chronicler does not editorialize; the raw data is offered and the reader who knows Deuteronomy 17 makes the assessment. The chariot-cities (ʿārê hāreḵeḇ) mark a shift from the mobile warrior-covenant people to a settled power-state — a shift with long-term consequences for covenant faithfulness.
15: wayyittēn hammelek et-hakkesep wᵉʾet-hazzāhāḇ bîrûšālaim kāʾăḇānîm — ‘The king made silver and gold as common in Jerusalem as stones.’ The hyperbolic comparison — precious metals as common as stones — signals the fulfilment of the second part of YHWH’s promise (v. 12): wisdom brings wealth as consequence. wᵉhāʾărāzîm nātan kāšiqmîm (and cedar as plentiful as sycamore trees in the lowland) — the cedar-sycamore comparison establishes a local benchmark: in the Shephelah, sycamore (šiqmāh) was the common timber; in Jerusalem under Solomon, the luxury cedar of Lebanon replaced it as the default construction material. Prosperity has normalized the extraordinary.
16: mōṣāʾ hassûsîm ʾăšer lišlōmōh mimmiṣrayim ûmiqwēh — ‘Solomon’s horses were imported from Egypt and Kue (Cilicia).’ qwh (Kue) is the ancient name for the Cilician plain in southern Anatolia — a region known for horse-breeding in the Iron Age. The royal traders (sōḥᵉrê hammelek) obtained horses from Kue at a standard price, establishing a royal trade monopoly. The Egypt-Kue trade axis placed Israel at the intersection of two major commercial routes, consistent with the wisdom-and-prosperity theme; but it also entangled Israel in Egyptian military commerce, the very dependency Deuteronomy warned against.
17: wayaʿălû wayyōṣiʾû mimmitrayim merkāḇāh bᵉšēš meʾôt kesef wᵉsûs baḥămiššîm ûmeʾāh — ‘A chariot from Egypt cost six hundred shekels of silver, and a horse a hundred and fifty.’ The price-list is a commercial record embedded in the narrative — the Chronicler (following 1 Kgs 10:28-29) preserves actual trade data. 600 shekels per chariot, 150 per horse — the ratio implies four horses per chariot as the standard unit (4 × 150 = 600). ûḵen lᵉkol-malkhê haḥittîm ûmalkhê ʾărām (similarly for all the kings of the Hittites and Aram) — Solomon’s trading network made Israel the intermediary for regional arms commerce, a geopolitical role that generated wealth but entangled the covenant people in the political-military systems of surrounding nations.
